simple_explanation: A person you know well and like.
daily_life_use: Used to talk about people you spend free time with.
spoken_use: He's my best friend.
natural_native_use: We've been friends for years.
examples: I am meeting a friend for lunch.
She is a good friend of mine.
common_mistakes: Saying 'He is my friend' when you mean 'He is just someone I know'.
hindi_confusion_correction: In English, a 'friend' is closer than just an 'acquaintance' (जान-पहचान वाला).

simple_explanation: A group of parents and children living together.
daily_life_use: Used to talk about your relatives living with you or close to you.
spoken_use: How is your family doing?
natural_native_use: I come from a big family.
examples: My family lives in Delhi.
We are having a family dinner tonight.
common_mistakes: Saying 'My family members are...' instead of 'My family is...'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Family takes a singular verb in American English (The family is going).

simple_explanation: A person who lives near or next to you.
daily_life_use: Used when talking about people living in your street or building.
spoken_use: My neighbor is very helpful.
natural_native_use: We have friendly neighbors.
examples: I borrowed some sugar from my neighbor.
Our neighbors are having a party.
common_mistakes: Spelling it 'neighbour' is British, 'neighbor' is American. Both are fine.
hindi_confusion_correction: Don't confuse with 'roommate' (who shares your room/house).

simple_explanation: A person who is invited to visit someone's home or a place.
daily_life_use: Used when having people over for dinner, functions, or staying at hotels.
spoken_use: We have guests coming over today.
natural_native_use: Be our guest.
examples: Please offer some tea to the guests.
He was a guest at our wedding.
common_mistakes: Saying 'He is my house guest' when 'He is my guest' is enough.
hindi_confusion_correction: In India, guests are treated like gods (अतिथि देवो भव), but in English, 'guest' is a simple descriptive word.
formal_informal_note: Slightly formal, 'friends' is used if they are close.

simple_explanation: A person connected by blood or marriage.
daily_life_use: Used to describe aunts, uncles, cousins, etc.
spoken_use: A lot of my relatives live in Mumbai.
natural_native_use: We're visiting relatives this weekend.
examples: She has no close relatives left.
It was a gathering of friends and relatives.
common_mistakes: Saying 'My relations' instead of 'My relatives' when talking about people.
hindi_confusion_correction: 'Relation' means 'रिश्ता' (the connection), 'Relative' means 'रिश्तेदार' (the person).

simple_explanation: Someone you do not know.
daily_life_use: Used to warn kids or to talk about unknown people.
spoken_use: Don't talk to strangers.
natural_native_use: A total stranger helped me find my way.
examples: I sat next to a stranger on the bus.
It's hard to open up to a stranger.
common_mistakes: Using 'stranger' for someone acting weird. Use 'strange person' for that.
hindi_confusion_correction: Stranger only means 'unknown person', not 'foreigner'.
formal_informal_note: Can be used formally and informally.

simple_explanation: A person that you work with in a profession or business.
daily_life_use: Used mainly in professional and office settings.
spoken_use: I am having lunch with a colleague.
natural_native_use: He's a former colleague of mine.
examples: My colleagues supported me on this project.
She gets along well with her colleagues.
common_mistakes: Saying 'co-worker' and 'colleague' interchangeably; they are similar, but 'colleague' sounds more professional.
hindi_confusion_correction: Don't say 'office friend' when referring to a professional relationship; use 'colleague'.
formal_informal_note: Formal. In informal speech, people often use 'co-worker'.

simple_explanation: A person who is in charge of a worker or organization.
daily_life_use: Used at work to refer to the person you report to.
spoken_use: I need to ask my boss for a day off.
natural_native_use: My boss is quite strict.
examples: The boss called a meeting.
She is her own boss now.
common_mistakes: Using 'Boss' to address a friend or shopkeeper (common in India, incorrect in standard English).
hindi_confusion_correction: Only use for actual managers/employers, not as slang for 'sir'.

simple_explanation: A person who takes part in an undertaking with another, or a spouse.
daily_life_use: Used for a business associate or a husband/wife/romantic partner.
spoken_use: My partner and I are starting a business.
natural_native_use: She lives with her partner.
examples: He is a partner in a law firm.
I'm looking for a tennis partner.
common_mistakes: Using it only for business when it is widely used for romantic relationships in Western English.
hindi_confusion_correction: It doesn't always mean business; it's the most common way to say 'husband/wife/boyfriend/girlfriend' politely.
formal_informal_note: Versatile; can be formal (business) or informal (relationship).

simple_explanation: A person who is actively opposed or hostile to someone.
daily_life_use: Rare in daily life, often used metaphorically or in games/stories.
spoken_use: He has made many enemies in politics.
natural_native_use: Time is our worst enemy right now.
examples: The soldiers fought the enemy.
Sugar is the enemy of weight loss.
common_mistakes: Using 'enemy' for someone you just dislike. Use 'someone I don't get along with' instead.
hindi_confusion_correction: In daily life, we rarely say 'enemy' for people we hate; it's too strong.
formal_informal_note: Can sound dramatic in everyday conversation.

simple_explanation: Having a good, generous, and friendly nature.
daily_life_use: Used to describe someone who helps others.
spoken_use: It is very kind of you to help.
natural_native_use: She has a very kind heart.
examples: Always be kind to animals.
He gave me a kind smile.
common_mistakes: Saying 'He is kind person' instead of 'He is a kind person'.
hindi_confusion_correction: 'Kind' means both 'दयालु' (adjective) and 'प्रकार' (noun, like 'what kind?').

simple_explanation: Not polite; offensive or bad-mannered.
daily_life_use: Used when someone talks back badly or lacks manners.
spoken_use: Why are you being so rude?
natural_native_use: It's rude to point at people.
examples: The waiter was very rude to us.
I didn't mean to be rude.
common_mistakes: Using 'rude' for someone who is just angry. Rude specifically means lacking manners.
hindi_confusion_correction: Rude translates exactly to 'बदतमीज़'.
formal_informal_note: Used in all contexts to criticize behavior.

simple_explanation: Showing good manners and respect for others.
daily_life_use: Used to praise children or describe professional behavior.
spoken_use: He is a very polite young man.
natural_native_use: Please try to be polite to the guests.
examples: It is polite to say 'thank you'.
She gave a polite refusal.
common_mistakes: Saying 'He has polite' instead of 'He is polite'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Polite is an adjective (विनम्र). The noun form is 'politeness' (विनम्रता).
formal_informal_note: A highly valued trait in formal settings.

simple_explanation: Believing that you are better or more important than other people.
daily_life_use: Used to describe someone who shows off and looks down on others.
spoken_use: He is too arrogant to admit his mistake.
natural_native_use: I can't stand her arrogant attitude.
examples: The actor came across as very arrogant.
Don't be so arrogant just because you won.
common_mistakes: Confusing 'arrogant' with 'confident'. Confidence is positive, arrogance is negative.
hindi_confusion_correction: 'Proud' can be good (मुझे तुम पर गर्व है), but 'arrogant' is always bad (घमंडी).

simple_explanation: Kind and pleasant towards others.
daily_life_use: Used to describe someone easy to talk to.
spoken_use: Everyone in this town is so friendly.
natural_native_use: She gave me a friendly smile.
examples: Our new neighbors are very friendly.
He has a friendly nature.
common_mistakes: Using 'friendly' as an adverb. It is an adjective. Say 'in a friendly way'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Friendly describes a person's nature, not just if they are your friend.

simple_explanation: Nervous or uncomfortable around other people.
daily_life_use: Used to describe kids who hide behind parents or adults who don't talk much.
spoken_use: Don't be shy, come and say hello.
natural_native_use: He is too shy to speak in public.
examples: She was a very shy child.
I felt shy when everyone looked at me.
common_mistakes: Pronouncing it as 'she'. It rhymes with 'my'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Shy is a personality trait. If you are embarrassed by a mistake, use 'embarrassed', not 'shy'.

simple_explanation: Fond of talking a lot.
daily_life_use: Used for someone who chatters continuously.
spoken_use: She is a very talkative person.
natural_native_use: He becomes talkative when he's nervous.
examples: I sat next to a talkative passenger on the train.
My daughter is very talkative.
common_mistakes: Saying 'He is too much talker' instead of 'He is talkative'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Exact match for 'बातूनी'. Another word is 'chatty'.
formal_informal_note: Informal. For a formal negative sense, you might use 'loquacious'.

simple_explanation: Making little or no noise; a person who speaks very little.
daily_life_use: Used to tell someone to stop talking or to describe a calm person.
spoken_use: Please be quiet in the library.
natural_native_use: He's a very quiet guy.
examples: The class was surprisingly quiet.
She is quiet, but very smart.
common_mistakes: Spelling it as 'quite' (which means 'very'). Remember: q-u-i-E-t.
hindi_confusion_correction: 'Quiet' can mean both no sound (चुप) and a calm personality (शांत स्वभाव).

simple_explanation: Intelligent, or showing quick and clever thinking.
daily_life_use: Used to compliment someone's intelligence or their clothing.
spoken_use: That was a smart move.
natural_native_use: She is a very smart cookie.
examples: He is the smartest student in the class.
You look very smart in that suit.
common_mistakes: Using 'smart' only for appearance (looking good). In modern English, it mostly means intelligent.
hindi_confusion_correction: In India, 'smart' often means good-looking. Native speakers usually use it for 'intelligent'.

simple_explanation: Unwilling to work or use energy.
daily_life_use: Used when someone avoids doing their chores or duties.
spoken_use: Don't be so lazy, get up and help!
natural_native_use: I'm feeling a bit lazy today.
examples: He is too lazy to cook.
We had a lazy afternoon at the beach.
common_mistakes: Saying 'He is feeling laziness' instead of 'He is feeling lazy'.
hindi_confusion_correction: A perfect translation for 'आलसी'.
formal_informal_note: Can be an insult or just a casual description of a mood.

simple_explanation: Putting a lot of effort and care into work.
daily_life_use: Used to praise someone's dedication to their job or studies.
spoken_use: She is a hardworking student.
natural_native_use: He's very hardworking and reliable.
examples: My father is a very hardworking man.
Hardworking employees usually get promoted.
common_mistakes: Spelling it as two words 'hard working'. It's usually one word.
hindi_confusion_correction: Means the same as 'मेहनती'.
formal_informal_note: Excellent word to use in job interviews or formal recommendations.

simple_explanation: Telling the truth and not stealing or cheating.
daily_life_use: Used to describe trustworthy people or true statements.
spoken_use: To be honest, I didn't like the food.
natural_native_use: He is an honest man.
examples: Give me your honest opinion.
She is honest about her past.
common_mistakes: Pronouncing the 'H'. The 'H' is silent! Say 'On-ist', not 'Hon-ist'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Using 'an honest' is correct because it starts with an 'O' sound. (He is an honest boy).
formal_informal_note: Common in both formal and casual English.

simple_explanation: Not genuine; pretending to be something one is not.
daily_life_use: Used for counterfiet items, but also for people who act nice but aren't.
spoken_use: I don't like her, she's so fake.
natural_native_use: He gave a fake smile.
examples: She has a lot of fake friends.
He was using a fake ID.
common_mistakes: Using 'duplicate' for a person. Always use 'fake' for an ungenuine person.
hindi_confusion_correction: In Hindi 'नकली' means a fake item. For people, it means two-faced (दोगला/दिखावटी).
formal_informal_note: Informal when used for people's personalities.

simple_explanation: Giving firm and constant support or allegiance to a person or institution.
daily_life_use: Used for good friends, long-term employees, or pets like dogs.
spoken_use: He is a very loyal friend.
natural_native_use: She remained loyal to the company.
examples: Dogs are known to be loyal animals.
They are loyal supporters of the team.
common_mistakes: Confusing 'loyal' with 'royal' (शाही).
hindi_confusion_correction: Loyal perfectly translates to वफादार.
formal_informal_note: Highly respected trait in formal and personal contexts.

simple_explanation: Caring only about oneself and not about other people.
daily_life_use: Used when someone doesn't share or thinks only of their own gain.
spoken_use: Don't be so selfish, share your toys.
natural_native_use: It was selfish of him to eat all the pizza.
examples: She is too selfish to care about others.
He made a selfish decision.
common_mistakes: Using 'mean' and 'selfish' interchangeably. 'Mean' is being cruel; 'selfish' is keeping things for yourself.
hindi_confusion_correction: Perfect translation for 'मतलबी'.
formal_informal_note: A strong negative word, use carefully.

hindi_meaning: ख्याल रखने वाला, परवाह करने वाला
simple_explanation: Displaying kindness and concern for others.
daily_life_use: Used to describe loving mothers, nurses, or good friends.
spoken_use: He is a very caring husband.
natural_native_use: She has a caring nature.
examples: Thank you for being such a caring friend.
The hospital staff was very caring.
common_mistakes: Saying 'He has caring' instead of 'He is caring'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Comes from the verb 'care' (परवाह करना).

simple_explanation: Demanding that rules concerning behavior are obeyed.
daily_life_use: Used to describe tough teachers, parents, or rules.
spoken_use: My parents are very strict.
natural_native_use: Our teacher is strict but fair.
examples: You need to follow a strict diet.
They are very strict about time.
common_mistakes: Saying 'He is strictly' when describing a person. 'Strictly' is an adverb (strictly prohibited).
hindi_confusion_correction: Translates directly to 'सख्त'.

simple_explanation: Making you smile or laugh.
daily_life_use: Used for jokes, comedians, or friends who make you laugh.
spoken_use: That joke was really funny.
natural_native_use: He's a funny guy.
examples: She told a funny story.
I didn't find that funny at all.
common_mistakes: Using 'fun' and 'funny' as the same thing. 'Fun' = enjoyable (a roller coaster). 'Funny' = makes you laugh (a clown).
hindi_confusion_correction: If someone is enjoyable to hang out with, they are 'fun'. If they tell jokes, they are 'funny'.
formal_informal_note: Informal. Can also mean 'strange' (My stomach feels funny).

simple_explanation: Not joking; demanding careful consideration.
daily_life_use: Used for people who don't smile much, or severe situations.
spoken_use: Are you serious?
natural_native_use: He is a very serious student.
examples: This is a serious matter.
She looked very serious during the meeting.
common_mistakes: Saying 'Are you in serious?' instead of 'Are you serious?'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Can mean both 'severe' (serious injury) and 'not joking' (गंभीर इंसान).

simple_explanation: To come together with someone at the same place.
daily_life_use: Used when making plans with friends or being introduced to someone new.
spoken_use: Nice to meet you.
natural_native_use: Let's meet up for coffee later.
examples: We are meeting at the station.
I met her last year.
common_mistakes: Saying 'I met with my friend'. Just say 'I met my friend'. ('Meet with' is for formal meetings).
hindi_confusion_correction: In Hindi we say 'मैं राम से मिला' (with/to), but in English it's direct: 'I met Ram'.

simple_explanation: To speak in order to give information or express ideas.
daily_life_use: Used for conversations, gossiping, or discussions.
spoken_use: We need to talk.
natural_native_use: I was just talking to him.
examples: She talks a lot.
Let's talk about the project.
common_mistakes: Saying 'He is talking with me' instead of 'He is talking to me' (both are fine, but 'to' is more common).
hindi_confusion_correction: The 'L' in talk is completely silent. It's 'tawk', not 'tall-k'.

simple_explanation: To express disagreement in a loud or angry way.
daily_life_use: Used when couples or friends have a fight with words.
spoken_use: Don't argue with me.
natural_native_use: They are always arguing over money.
examples: The kids are arguing about the game.
I don't want to argue anymore.
common_mistakes: Confusing 'argue' with 'fight'. Arguing is with words, fighting is usually physical.
hindi_confusion_correction: Perfect translation for 'बहस करना'.

simple_explanation: To have the same opinion about something.
daily_life_use: Used to say 'yes, you are right' in conversations.
spoken_use: I completely agree with you.
natural_native_use: We finally agreed on a price.
examples: Do you agree?
I agree to your terms.
common_mistakes: Saying 'I am agree'. The correct phrase is 'I agree'. 'Agree' is an action verb, not an adjective.
hindi_confusion_correction: Hindi speakers often use 'am' because of 'मैं सहमत हूँ' (am), but in English, 'agree' is the main verb.

simple_explanation: To say sorry for something that you have done wrong.
daily_life_use: Used when you make a mistake and want to fix the relationship.
spoken_use: I apologize for my mistake.
natural_native_use: You should apologize to her.
examples: He apologized for being late.
I want to apologize for what I said.
common_mistakes: Saying 'I apologize you'. You must say 'I apologize TO you'.
hindi_confusion_correction: More formal than just saying 'Sorry'.
formal_informal_note: A polite, slightly formal way to say sorry.

simple_explanation: To stop feeling angry or resentful toward someone for an offense.
daily_life_use: Used after an apology to mend a relationship.
spoken_use: Please forgive me.
natural_native_use: I can't forgive him for what he did.
examples: She completely forgave him.
It takes time to forgive.
common_mistakes: Using 'forgave' (past tense) when you should use 'forgiven' (past participle) - 'I have forgiven him'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Opposite of 'punish'.

simple_explanation: To believe that someone is good, honest, and reliable.
daily_life_use: Crucial word for deep relationships and business.
spoken_use: I trust you completely.
natural_native_use: Trust me, this will work.
examples: You can trust him with your money.
Trust is important in a marriage.
common_mistakes: Saying 'I do trust on him'. Just say 'I trust him'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Unlike Hindi 'पर भरोसा', English doesn't need 'on' after trust as a verb.
formal_informal_note: Essential word in all forms of English.

simple_explanation: To give help or assistance to someone.
daily_life_use: Used for emotional backing, financial help, or cheering a team.
spoken_use: Thanks for your support.
natural_native_use: My family always supports me.
examples: Which team do you support?
I will support your decision.
common_mistakes: Confusing it with 'help'. 'Support' is ongoing backing; 'help' is usually a one-time task.
hindi_confusion_correction: Means both 'साथ देना' (verb) and 'समर्थन' (noun).

simple_explanation: A feeling of deep admiration for someone.
daily_life_use: Used for elders, teachers, or someone's good qualities.
spoken_use: You should respect your elders.
natural_native_use: I have a lot of respect for her.
examples: He treated me with respect.
I respect your opinion.
common_mistakes: Saying 'give respect to him' is okay, but simply saying 'respect him' is better English.
hindi_confusion_correction: Unlike Hindi where we 'give' respect (इज़्ज़त देना), in English 'respect' itself is the verb.

simple_explanation: To refuse to take notice of or acknowledge someone.
daily_life_use: Used when you deliberately don't look at or listen to someone.
spoken_use: Why are you ignoring me?
natural_native_use: Just ignore the haters.
examples: She ignored my messages.
Don't ignore the warning signs.
common_mistakes: Pronouncing it as 'ig-nore-ance'. The 'ance' makes it a noun (ignorance = अज्ञानता), 'ignore' is the verb.
hindi_confusion_correction: Perfect translation for 'अनदेखा करना' or 'नज़रअंदाज़ करना'.

simple_explanation: Forming one's features into a pleased, kind, or amused expression.
daily_life_use: Used to describe looking happy.
spoken_use: She has a beautiful smile.
natural_native_use: He smiled at me.
examples: Always start your day with a smile.
They were smiling and laughing.
common_mistakes: Saying 'He smiled to me' instead of 'He smiled AT me'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Smile means मुस्कुराना. Laugh means हँसना (with sound). Don't mix them.
